card.indications: Adjunct treatment of elevated LDL cholesterol, relief of itching associated with partial biliary obstruction, and selected off-label uses such as bile-acid diarrhea when prescribed.
card.mechanism: Binds bile acids in the intestine to form a nonabsorbable complex excreted in feces. Increased conversion of hepatic cholesterol to bile acids lowers LDL cholesterol and reduces circulating pruritogenic bile acids.
card.dosing: A common starting dose is 4 g once or twice daily, titrated according to response. Maintenance commonly ranges from 8 to 16 g daily in divided doses; the usual maximum is 24 g daily. Increase slowly to reduce constipation.
card.routeAdministration: Never take the powder dry. Mix thoroughly with at least 2 to 6 ounces of water, juice, noncarbonated beverage, soup, or soft food and drink promptly. Take other oral medications at least 1 hour before or 4 to 6 hours after cholestyramine unless a clinician gives different instructions.
card.contraindications: Complete biliary obstruction and hypersensitivity to product components. Use caution with severe constipation, bowel obstruction risk, hypertriglyceridemia, dysphagia, or phenylketonuria if a flavored product contains phenylalanine.
card.warnings: Can cause or worsen constipation, fecal impaction, hemorrhoids, and rarely bowel obstruction. Long-term use may impair absorption of vitamins A, D, E, K, folate, and many medications. It may increase triglycerides and rarely cause hyperchloremic acidosis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, bloating, abdominal discomfort, nausea, flatulence, heartburn, and unpleasant texture or taste.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Bowel obstruction, fecal impaction, severe hypertriglyceridemia, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency with bleeding or bone disease, and hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.
card.interactions: Binds many drugs, including warfarin, thyroid hormone, digoxin, thiazides, some beta blockers, corticosteroids, and certain antibiotics. It can reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Separate dosing carefully.
card.monitoring: Monitor lipid panel including triglycerides, bowel pattern, abdominal symptoms, medication timing, INR when warfarin is used, and signs of vitamin deficiency during long-term therapy. Assess hydration and dental hygiene because the resin can affect teeth if held in the mouth.
card.patientTeaching: Mix completely and never swallow dry powder. Increase fluids and fiber gradually if allowed. Separate other medicines as directed. Report severe constipation, inability to pass gas, abdominal swelling, unusual bleeding, or worsening weakness.
card.nutrition: Increase fluids and dietary fiber unless restricted. A heart-healthy, lower-saturated-fat diet improves lipid response. Long-term therapy may require supplementation of vitamins A, D, E, K, or folate under clinical supervision.

card.indications: Treatment of duodenal and gastric ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux disease, pathologic hypersecretory conditions, and short-term relief or prevention of heartburn depending on prescription or OTC product.
card.mechanism: Competitively blocks histamine H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated gastric-acid secretion and lowering pepsin output.
card.dosing: Prescription dosing varies by indication. Common adult regimens include 400 mg twice daily or 800 mg at bedtime for ulcer disease, and divided dosing for reflux or hypersecretory conditions. OTC products use lower doses. Reduce the dose or extend the interval in renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Bedtime dosing is often used for ulcer therapy. IV doses should be diluted and administered at the recommended rate; rapid IV administration can cause hypotension or arrhythmias.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cimetidine or other H2 blockers. Use caution in renal or hepatic impairment, older adults, patients with confusion risk, and patients taking drugs with a narrow therapeutic index.
card.warnings: Can cause confusion, agitation, hallucinations, or somnolence, especially in older or acutely ill patients and those with renal impairment. It inhibits multiple hepatic enzymes and can cause clinically important drug interactions. Long-term therapy may mask gastric malignancy or contribute to vitamin B12 deficiency.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, dizziness, diarrhea, constipation, fatigue, rash, and mild elevation of liver enzymes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe confusion or delirium, bradycardia or hypotension with rapid IV use, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, hepatitis, interstitial nephritis, anaphylaxis, and rare gynecomastia or sexual dysfunction with prolonged high-dose therapy.
card.interactions: Inhibits CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4 and can increase concentrations of warfarin, phenytoin, theophylline, lidocaine, some benzodiazepines, and other drugs. It can reduce absorption of medications that require an acidic stomach environment.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom relief, occult or overt GI bleeding, renal and hepatic function, mental status, CBC when clinically indicated, and serum levels or effects of interacting drugs. Reassess persistent or recurrent symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Take only for the recommended duration. Report black stools, vomiting blood, persistent abdominal pain, confusion, breast enlargement, or unusual bruising. Check with a pharmacist before starting another medication because cimetidine has many interactions.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Avoid foods, alcohol, caffeine, or tobacco that worsen reflux or ulcer symptoms. Long-term users at risk for deficiency may need vitamin B12 assessment.
